2021 · CVPR · Benchmarks & Evals

Natural Adversarial Examples

Curates two real-photo test sets — IMAGENET-A (7,500 images) and IMAGENET-O (2,000 images) — with a single adversarial-filtration recipe: keep only unmodified photos that a fixed ResNet-50 ensemble already gets wrong (or wrongly overconfident on). No pixels are perturbed, yet a DenseNet-121's accuracy collapses from ≥90% to about 2% on IMAGENET-A, and the failures transfer reliably to unseen CNNs and even Vision Transformers.

Dan Hendrycks (UC Berkeley), Kevin Zhao (University of Washington), Steven Basart (UChicago), Jacob Steinhardt, Dawn Song (UC Berkeley)
venue CVPR 2021 citations 1,984 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By 2019, "hard examples for ImageNet classifiers" meant one of a few narrow things. Most work centered on ℓp adversarial examples — inputs perturbed by a nearly worst-case, imperceptibly small distortion crafted specifically to fool a model. But Su et al. (2018) had already shown that most ℓp adversarial examples crafted against one model barely transfer outside that model's own family — a narrow, model-specific threat model, not evidence that vision systems share weaknesses. A second line of work measured robustness to synthetic distribution shift — the same authors' own IMAGENET-C benchmark applies 75 algorithmically generated corruption conditions (noise, blur, weather, digital) to clean images — but a corruption pipeline is still an artificial stand-in for what a deployed model actually meets in the wild. A third, IMAGENET-R (a later paper by several of the same authors), renders ImageNet classes as art, cartoons, and sculptures — real images, but of a stylized rendition, not the plain photograph a camera would capture.

Meanwhile, evidence was mounting that the standard ImageNet test set itself was too easy to be a meaningful stress test. Recht et al.'s 2019 ImageNetV2 paper observed that ImageNet test examples "tend to be simple, clear, close-up images" — and Recht et al. found that matching the original test set's difficulty required Mechanical Turk annotators to select the easiest, most obvious replacement images, so the accuracy drop on ImageNetV2 came out under 3.6% (per Engstrom et al.'s later re-analysis). Geirhos et al.'s "shortcut learning" argument compounded this: models can reach high accuracy on such easy test sets by leaning on spurious cues — a cow classified by its green-pasture background rather than the animal — that the standard benchmark never penalizes. What was missing was a test set of real, unmodified photographs that are hard for exactly the reason models are supposed to be tested on: because they expose genuine, shared classifier blind spots, not because they are adversarially perturbed, synthetically corrupted, or stylistically transformed. No one had shown that such a "naturally occurring adversarial example" category even existed at scale, let alone that it would transfer across model families the way the paper's title claims.

// how it works

The method

The recipe is adversarial filtration applied to real photographs rather than to model weights or pixels: download a large pool of candidate images tagged to an ImageNet class, then repeatedly throw away every image a fixed classifier gets right, until only the images it reliably gets wrong remain. This idea was already common in NLP — HellaSwag, Winogrande, and other "adversarial datasets" sieve out examples solved by simple spurious cues — but the authors note it had not previously been applied to curating an image dataset, and NLP's version keeps the easiest examples while this paper's version keeps the hardest and discards the middle.

For IMAGENET-A, the pipeline restricts to a 200-class subset of ImageNet-1K chosen so that confusions between them are "egregious" (mistaking a Persian cat for a candle, not a Norwich terrier for a Norfolk terrier), and excludes rare, coarse, or overlapping classes. Images are pulled from iNaturalist, Flickr, and DuckDuckGo — not just the small existing ImageNet test set, which the authors found too small to yield hard examples after filtering. Two ResNet-50 classifiers (one fine-tuned on the 200-class subset, one using 200 of its 1,000 original logits) perform 10-crop classification on every candidate; an image is discarded if any crop is classified correctly, or if either model assigns more than 15% confidence to the true class. A second filtering pass caps each confusion pair (e.g. "Persian cat → lynx") at 15 occurrences, so the final set has a dense, varied confusion matrix rather than a handful of repeated easy confusions. Graduate students — who memorized the full 200-class list to avoid multi-label ambiguity — then manually selected the final, visually clear, single-class images from the shortlist. As a concrete yield example: 81,413 dragonfly images downloaded from iNaturalist survive the ResNet-50 filter down to 8,925, then the diversified shortlist down to 1,452, from which 80 are manually selected for the final dataset. The result is 7,500 adversarially filtered images across 200 classes.

The companion dataset, IMAGENET-O, applies the same idea to out-of-distribution (OOD) detection rather than classification. It starts from ImageNet-22K with the 1,000 ImageNet-1K classes removed, then keeps only the images a ResNet-50 classifies as an in-distribution class with high confidence — i.e., images the standard "negative maximum softmax probability" OOD detector (Hendrycks & Gimpel, 2017) mistakenly treats as ordinary in-distribution inputs. The authors chose ImageNet-22K specifically because it was collected the same way as ImageNet-1K, so the distribution shift is purely semantic (new object categories) rather than a confound of unrelated non-semantic factors like image resolution or JPEG artifacting. The result is 2,000 curated anomalies — the same per-class count as ImageNetV2 — evaluated by AUPR (area under the precision-recall curve) against a random-chance floor of about 16.67%. IMAGENET-A tests classification under an input-distribution shift; IMAGENET-O tests uncertainty estimation under a label-distribution shift.

A sanity check the authors run before trusting either dataset: the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) between the ImageNet validation and test sets is about 0.99 (near-identical distributions, as expected), while the FID between IMAGENET-A and the ImageNet validation set is 50.40 — confirming a large, genuine distribution shift rather than a labeling artifact. Crucially, the images are still solvable: the authors estimate their own graduate-student annotators reach roughly 90% human accuracy on IMAGENET-A, meaning the dataset targets a real classifier blind spot rather than genuinely ambiguous or mislabeled inputs.

// what they found

The results

The headline number: on the same 200 classes, ordinary classifiers usually score ≥90% top-1 accuracy on standard ImageNet images, but a DenseNet-121 collapses to about 2% on IMAGENET-A — an approximately 90-point drop, and none of the five architectures tested in Figure 2 (AlexNet, SqueezeNet, VGG-19, DenseNet-121, ResNet-50, none of which were used to build the dataset) clear roughly 3% accuracy. For context, the authors note this is a far larger degradation than the sibling ImageNetV2 replication study, where Engstrom et al. estimate the accuracy drop from ImageNet to ImageNetV2 at under 3.6% — "model accuracy can decrease by over 50% with IMAGENET-A," in the paper's own words. On IMAGENET-O, every tested model's OOD-detection AUPR sits within a few points of the roughly 16.67% random-chance line — the maximum-softmax-probability detector, a longstanding OOD baseline, is essentially defeated.

The expanded architecture sweep (Table 1 in the paper) shows accuracy climbing with scale and design, but staying low in absolute terms:

ModelIMAGENET-A acc.IMAGENET-O AUPR
AlexNet1.77%15.44%
ResNet-502.17%16.20%
ResNet-1526.05%18.00%
ResNeXt-101 (32×8d)10.2%20.51%
Res2Net-50 (v1b)14.59%19.50%
Res2Net-152 (v1b)22.4%23.90%
DeiT-small (ViT)19.1%20.9%
DeiT-base (ViT)28.2%24.8%

Data augmentation, by contrast, barely moves the number. Starting from a from-scratch ResNet-50 baseline of 2.17%: adversarial training (Wong et al., 2020) actually decreases accuracy to 1.68%; Style Transfer training (Geirhos et al.'s texture-debiasing method, which substantially helps on synthetic corruptions) adds only 0.13 points; AugMix reaches 3.8%; Cutout 4.4%; Moment Exchange (MoEx) 5.5%; Mixup 6.6%; and the best of the pack, CutMix, reaches 7.3% — about a 5-point gain at most over the untouched baseline. More labeled data fares similarly poorly at ordinary scale: pretraining on Places365 actually drops accuracy to 1.56%, and fine-tuning a baseline ResNet-50 on 80% of IMAGENET-A itself shows no clear improvement on the held-out 20% (both top-1 and top-5 accuracy stay below 2% and 5% respectively). Only a full order-of-magnitude more labeled pretraining data — ImageNet-21K, roughly 14 million images across 21,000 classes — buys a real jump, to 11.41% accuracy (a 9.24-point increase), with IMAGENET-O AUPR improving from 16.20% to 21.86% (though the authors flag this OOD gain as less meaningful, since ImageNet-21K itself overlaps with IMAGENET-O's source images).

Architecture changes are the one lever that clearly works. Simply scaling ResNet-50 → ResNet-152 lifts accuracy from 2.17% to 6.05%; grouped convolutions (ResNeXt-50, 32×4d) reach 4.81%; Squeeze-and-Excitation self-attention lifts ResNet-50 to 6.17%; and Res2Net's multi-scale residual blocks give the largest single gain in the paper — 14.59% for a ResNet-50-sized Res2Net-50, climbing to 22.4% for a ResNet-152-sized Res2Net-152. Vision Transformers transfer-fool just as reliably as CNNs: DeiT-small reaches only 19.1% despite having a similar parameter count to Res2Net-50's 14.6% (partly attributable to DeiT's built-in Mixup training), while the larger DeiT-base reaches 28.2% accuracy and 24.8% AUPR — the best numbers reported anywhere in the paper, and still far from a ceiling.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

IMAGENET-A became one of the most widely reported "hard ImageNet" numbers in the robustness literature, and for good reason — the transfer evidence in Figure 3 (models never used to build the dataset still collapse to single-digit accuracy) is the paper's strongest argument that these are genuine, shared blind spots rather than an artifact of one specific classifier. But a few things are worth naming precisely, especially since the benchmark's headline framing ("natural adversarial examples") invites reading it as more model-agnostic than the construction actually guarantees.

First, the curation target is defined by, and only by, what fools ResNet-50. Both datasets are built by adversarially filtering against a small ensemble of ResNet-50 variants; the strong cross-model transfer result is real and important, but it is also expected to some degree, since CNN architectures of that era share broad inductive biases — heavy reliance on local texture and background co-occurrence, as the paper's own Grad-CAM heatmaps show below. A dataset defined as "whatever fools this one architecture family" risks encoding that family's specific spurious-cue habits into what counts as "hard," rather than a family-agnostic notion of difficulty — a concern the DeiT result partially, but only partially, answers, since DeiT-base still reaches under 30%.

Second, the manual curation step is a real, unaudited source of selection. Only a small fraction of the algorithmically filtered shortlist makes the final cut — for the dragonfly example the paper walks through, 80 of 1,452 shortlisted images (about 5.5%) were manually kept — and that selection was made by graduate-student curators with no reported inter-annotator agreement statistic, blinding protocol, or documented selection criteria beyond "valid, single-class, and high-quality." Curators could easily and unknowingly favor certain visually striking failure types (the background-texture-confusion examples make for compelling figures) over others, which would bias the released set toward the paper's own narrative about failure modes without that bias being measurable from the released data.

Third, "natural adversarial" is an operational category, not a formal one, and that is a genuine trade-off rather than a flaw to fix: unlike an ℓp adversarial example (a well-defined worst-case perturbation within a fixed budget), IMAGENET-A's difficulty boundary is whatever iNaturalist, Flickr, and DuckDuckGo happen to index and whatever a ResNet-50 happens to miss. That gives the dataset real-world face validity but no formal guarantee of coverage or stability — a different search index, a different filtering architecture, or a different curator team could plausibly yield a different 7,500 images with different failure-mode emphasis, and the paper offers no ablation varying the filtering model family to check how much the results would change.

Finally, this is exactly the kind of benchmark that saturates as architectures improve — the paper's own results already show this trajectory in miniature: architecture alone lifts accuracy from 2.17% (ResNet-50) to 28.2% (DeiT-base) with no additional data or fine-tuning. IMAGENET-A predates the CLIP/ViT-scale, web-pretrained era of vision models by roughly a year, and the paper does not (and could not) test any web-scale-pretrained backbone; subsequent large vision-language and foundation models are widely known to score much higher on IMAGENET-A than anything reported here, which narrows its power as a discriminating stress test for today's frontier vision systems even as it remains informative about the specific 2019–2021 architecture generation it targets.

baselines

Extensive and fair for its era — five held-out architectures in the core transfer test, plus an 18-row expanded sweep (Table 1) spanning AlexNet through Res2Net-152 and DeiT, none used to build the dataset. No confidence intervals or repeated curation runs are reported, however, so the sensitivity of the 7,500-image set to curator or filtering-model choice is untested.

contamination

Low risk by construction — IMAGENET-A/O images come from iNaturalist, Flickr, and DuckDuckGo rather than from held-out ImageNet splits, and the filtering step actively excludes anything a standard ResNet-50 already gets right. The larger risk is definitional, not leakage: the dataset is only as "hard" as ResNet-50's specific blind spots, so a model architecturally similar to the filtering classifiers may look artificially weak, and a very different model family (as DeiT partly shows) may look artificially strong relative to its true robustness.

what's missed

No ablation on filtering-model choice (would a non-ResNet ensemble curate a materially different 7,500 images?); no inter-annotator agreement or documented protocol for the manual "visually clear, single-class" curation step that keeps only ~5.5% of the algorithmic shortlist; and no web-scale-pretrained or vision-language backbone tested, so the paper cannot speak to how far its own headline gap has since closed.

would raise confidence

A published ablation curating a second version of IMAGENET-A against a non-ResNet filtering family to test how model-specific the difficulty really is; a documented curator-agreement statistic for the manual selection step; and periodic re-benchmarking against newer architectures and pretraining scales, since — as the paper's own architecture results already foreshadow — this is a benchmark built to be outgrown.

// bottom line

Natural Adversarial Examples earns its place as a landmark: it showed, convincingly, that real unmodified photographs — no perturbation, no synthetic corruption, no stylization — can reliably fool every CNN family and even Vision Transformers down to single-digit accuracy, and that neither data augmentation nor an order of magnitude more training data closes the gap the way better architecture does. But the benchmark's "hardness" is inseparable from the ResNet-50 ensemble and graduate-student curators that built it, its 200-class, egregious-error framing is itself a curation choice, and — as its own numbers already hint — it is a benchmark for a specific architecture generation that later, much larger vision models have likely substantially outgrown.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. One adversarial-filtration recipe, two benchmarks. IMAGENET-A (7,500 real photos, 200 classes) tests classification under natural distribution shift by keeping only images a fixed ResNet-50 ensemble gets wrong; IMAGENET-O (2,000 images from ImageNet-22K) tests OOD detection by keeping only out-of-class images the same detector wrongly treats as high-confidence in-distribution.
  2. The collapse is real and transfers. Standard classifiers scoring ≥90% on ordinary ImageNet images fall to about 2% (DenseNet-121) on IMAGENET-A — a much larger drop than the ImageNetV2 replication study's under-3.6% gap — and the failure transfers to five architectures never used to build the dataset, plus Vision Transformers, evidence these are shared blind spots rather than one model's quirk.
  3. Architecture, not augmentation or data scale, is what moved the needle in this paper. Data augmentation bought at most ~5 points (CutMix, 2.17%→7.3%) and adversarial training made things slightly worse; a full order of magnitude more pretraining data (ImageNet-21K) bought +9.24 points; but Res2Net's multi-scale blocks alone lifted accuracy to 14.59–22.4%, and DeiT-base (a Vision Transformer) reached the paper's best result, 28.2% — still far from solved, and a trajectory that foreshadows how much later, larger models would go on to close this exact gap.

Natural Adversarial Examples
Dan Hendrycks, Kevin Zhao, Steven Basart, Jacob Steinhardt, Dawn Song · 2021 · CVPR
